coredata.dc:title:
Artesunate carbon dots for attenuating periodontal inflammation and promoting bone regeneration
via
activation of AMPK

Wang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: Periodontitis, recognized as a chronic inflammatory condition, manifests through sustained gingival inflammation and gradual degradation of alveolar bone tissue. Without proper intervention, this pathological process may culminate in permanent tooth detachment. Consequently, therapeutic strategies for periodontitis primarily focus on mitigating inflammatory reactions and stimulating osseous tissue regeneration. Recent pharmacological investigations have highlighted artesunate (ART) as a compound exhibiting notable anti-inflammatory efficacy, though its clinical application is constrained by limited aqueous solubility and suboptimal pharmacokinetic properties. Carbon-based quantum dots, emerging as versatile nanoscale materials, demonstrate multiple biological functionalities. This research successfully synthesized artesunate-derived carbon dots (ACDs) through hydrothermal synthesis and comprehensively evaluated their dual therapeutic effects on inflammation suppression and bone formation enhancement in periodontal disease models. Experimental data revealed that ACDs exhibit improved hydrophilicity, favorable biocompatibility, and simultaneous anti-inflammatory/osteogenic capabilities. Mechanistically, ACDs activated the adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase (AMPK), subsequently inhibiting nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB) signaling transduction, which effectively reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion. AMPK pathway activation further upregulated expression patterns of osteogenic differentiation markers at both mRNA translation and protein synthesis levels, enhanced mineralization capacity in rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (rBMSCs), and increased calcified nodule formation. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that ACDs effectively counteract lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammatory responses while substantially improving bone regenerative processes through AMPK pathway modulation. This investigation suggests ACDs as a promising nanotechnology-based approach for periodontitis management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artesunate
Carbon dots
Periodontitis
AMPK
Osteogenesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impact of ACDs for anti-inflammatory effects on LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 and promoting osteogenesis on rBMSCs
ACDs ameliorated lipopolysaccharide LPS-induced inflammation and significantly facilitated bone regeneration via AMPK activation
ACDs reduce tissue damage in a rat model of ligation-induced periodontitis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of ACDs. (A) TEM image of ACDs. (B) Diameter distribution histogram of ACDs. (C) HRTEM image of ACDs. (D) FTIR spectra of ART (red) and ACDs (blue). (E) Normalized Abs (a.u.) (red line) and normalized PL (a.u.) (blue line) spectrum of ACDs. (F) XPS scan spectrum measurement of ACDs. (G) C 1 s spectrum of ACDs and ART. (H) N 1 s spectrum of ACDs and ART. (I) O 1 s spectrum of ACDs and ART. Abbreviations: ART, Artesunate.

Anti-inflammatory and osteogenic effects of ACDs. (A–C) mRNA expression levels of IL-1β (A), IL-6 (B), and TNF-α (C) in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages treated with ACDs or ART, as determined by qRT-PCR. (D) IL-1β protein secretion was quantified via ELISA in LPS-challenged RAW 264.7 cells following ACDs or ART administration. (E–H) Transcriptional profiles of osteogenic differentiation markers (ALP, COL1, RUNX2, and Sp7) in rBMSCs exposed to ACDs or ART, analyzed through qRT-PCR. (I) Temporal ALP enzymatic activity visualized by histochemical staining at days 7 and 14 post-induction. (J, K) Quantitative evaluation of ALP staining intensity at day 7 (J) and day 14 (K). (L) Calcium mineralization assessed by Alizarin Red S (ARS) staining at day 21. (M) Semi-quantitative analysis of ARS-stained mineralized nodules. Data represent mean values ± SD (triplicate experiments). Statistical significance: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. Abbreviations: Ctrl, negative control; ART, artesunate.

Mechanistic actions of ACDs in inflammation suppression via NF-κB inhibition and osteogenic enhancement through AMPK activation. (A–C) Immunoblot images (A) with β-actin-normalized densitometric quantification of inflammatory mediators TNF-α (B) and IL-6 (C). (D–G) Immunoblotting results (D) accompanied by β-actin-referenced densitometric histograms for AMPK (E) and NF-κB pathway components IκBα (F) and P65 (G). (H, I) Representative immunoblots (H) and quantitative analysis of p-AMPK levels normalized to β-actin (I). (J–N) Protein expression profiles (J) with β-actin-adjusted densitometry for osteogenic markers COL1 (K), RUNX2 (L), OCN (M), and DMP1 (N). Data represent mean ± SD (biological triplicate). Statistical significance: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 (parametric statistical analysis). Abbreviations: Ctrl, untreated control; ART, artesunate; NS, nonsignificant.

Therapeutic effects of ACDs on periodontal inflammation and bone regeneration in vivo. (A) Three-dimensional reconstructed micro-CT images demonstrating alveolar bone morphology across buccal, palatal, and sagittal planes. Bone resorption severity was quantified through linear measurements between CEJ (red lines) and ABC (blue lines). (B) Morphometric evaluation of CEJ-ABC distances. (C) BV/TV. (D) Tb. Sp. (E) Trabecular thickness (Tb. Th). (F) Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) stained sections (black arrow indicates inflammatory cells). (G) The corresponding quantification analysis of inflammatory cells. (H) Masson trichrome-stained sections demonstrating collagen fiber maturation. (I) Semi-quantitative assessment of mature collagen fibril density. Data expressed as mean ± SD (biological triplicate). Statistical significance markers: ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. Abbreviations: Ctrl, untreated controls; Perio, periodontitis model; ART, artesunate; NS, nonsignificant; M1, maxillary first molar; M2, maxillary second molar.

Osteogenesis and anti-inflammatory properties of ACDs in rats with ligature-induced periodontitis. (A) IHC staining images of periodontal tissues, including TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, OPN, and RUNX2. (B–F) The corresponding quantification analysis of the TNF-α (B), IL-6 (C), IL-1β (D), RUNX2 (E), and OPN (F) in IHC staining samples. The data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. Abbreviations: Ctrl, control; Perio, periodontitis; ART, artesunate.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of ACDs and the mechanism of inhibiting periodontal inflammation and promoting bone regeneration.

ce:title.ce:bold: Artesunate carbon dots for attenuating periodontal inflammation and promoting bone regeneration

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Hospital of Stomatology, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Periodontitis, recognized as a chronic inflammatory condition, manifests through sustained gingival inflammation and gradual degradation of alveolar bone tissue. Without proper intervention, this pathological process may culminate in permanent tooth detachment. Consequently, therapeutic strategies for periodontitis primarily focus on mitigating inflammatory reactions and stimulating osseous tissue regeneration. Recent pharmacological investigations have highlighted artesunate (ART) as a compound exhibiting notable anti-inflammatory efficacy, though its clinical application is constrained by limited aqueous solubility and suboptimal pharmacokinetic properties. Carbon-based quantum dots, emerging as versatile nanoscale materials, demonstrate multiple biological functionalities. This research successfully synthesized artesunate-derived carbon dots (ACDs) through hydrothermal synthesis and comprehensively evaluated their dual therapeutic effects on inflammation suppression and bone formation enhancement in periodontal disease models. Experimental data revealed that ACDs exhibit improved hydrophilicity, favorable biocompatibility, and simultaneous anti-inflammatory/osteogenic capabilities. Mechanistically, ACDs activated the adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase (AMPK), subsequently inhibiting nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB) signaling transduction, which effectively reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion. AMPK pathway activation further upregulated expression patterns of osteogenic differentiation markers at both mRNA translation and protein synthesis levels, enhanced mineralization capacity in rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (rBMSCs), and increased calcified nodule formation. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that ACDs effectively counteract lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammatory responses while substantially improving bone regenerative processes through AMPK pathway modulation. This investigation suggests ACDs as a promising nanotechnology-based approach for periodontitis management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Periodontitis represents a widespread, persistent oral disorder. Functioning as a principal structural element within the outer membrane architecture of Gram-negative microorganisms, lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is recognized as a critical contributor to the pathogenesis and progression of periodontal inflammatory disease. Through interaction with Toll-like receptors (TLRs) expressed by macrophages, LPS activates inflammatory pathways that foster the establishment of pro-inflammatory microenvironments [1,2]. Experimental investigations using rodent oral infection models revealed that pili from Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis) promote macrophage secretion of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 [3]. Chronic inflammatory signaling has been shown to impair the regenerative potential inherent in stem cell populations, with specific inhibitory effects observed on the repair processes of periodontal tissues [4]. Bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) are recognized for their self-renewal competence and multilineage differentiation capacity, enabling them to generate diverse cellular phenotypes [5]; however, exposure to a persistent inflammatory microenvironment markedly suppresses BMSC proliferation and differentiation, reduces the number of functional osteoblasts, and diminishes bone formation, thereby exacerbating periodontitis progression [6].

Currently, effective therapeutic strategies for periodontitis are limited to surgical repair, antibiotic therapy, and non-surgical mechanical debridement [7]. However, each approach presents significant drawbacks: long-term antibiotic use can induce resistance; mechanical removal of dental calculus fails to stimulate bone regeneration [8,9]; and surgical repair carries inherent procedural risks. Periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease, not only elicits localized inflammatory responses but also triggers progressive alveolar bone resorption, ultimately culminating in tooth loss. Therefore, developing therapeutic agents that combine anti-inflammatory activity with periodontal tissue regeneration capacity is essential to overcome these limitations.

In recent years, using traditional Chinese medicine and other natural products to treat periodontitis has emerged as a research focal point [10]. Artemisia annua, a botanical species historically utilized in traditional Chinese medicinal practices for fever reduction, underwent successful isolation of artemisinin through the groundbreaking work of Tu Youyou during the 1970s [11]. Artesunate (ART), a semi-synthetic compound derived from artemisinin, has found extensive clinical application as an antimalarial agent targeting Plasmodium falciparum infections. Beyond its primary antiparasitic function, this derivative demonstrates not only antimalarial efficacy but also marked anti-inflammatory characteristics [12], immunomodulatory effects [13], and prevents bone loss [14]. Furthermore, while traditional Chinese medicine extracts have demonstrated potential in periodontitis treatment, most studies have targeted either anti-inflammatory or antibacterial effects exclusively, with limited exploration of synergistic inflammation regulation and tissue regeneration. However, poor water solubility limits the therapeutic potential of ART, necessitating structural modification to enhance its efficacy.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have found extensive utilization in various scientific disciplines, among which carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant research attention owing to their distinctive characteristics [15,16]. CDs possess excellent biocompatibility, high water solubility, small size, capacity for multifunctional conjugation with other nanoparticles, and superior optical properties [17−20]. Various biologically functional CDs have been synthesized for anti-inflammatory [21], antibacterial [22], antiviral [23], and drug delivery applications [24]. Stability and extended drug half-life render CDs promising nanomaterials for medical applications [25]. For example, CDs synthesized from metronidazole via a hydrothermal method preserved the antibacterial activity of the parent compound while enhancing water solubility; the intrinsic fluorescence of these CDs also enables biological imaging [26]. To date, no studies have integrated traditional Chinese medicine derivatives exhibiting both anti-inflammatory and bone-regenerative activities into carbon dots, nor have any synthesized artesunate-derived carbon dots for application in periodontitis treatment.

This investigation employed a hydrothermal synthesis approach with ART as the precursor to develop artesunate-derived carbon dots (ACDs) for periodontal disease intervention. Comparative analyses revealed that ACDs demonstrate substantially improved aqueous solubility and biological compatibility relative to the parent ART compound. Experimental evaluations under controlled laboratory conditions indicated ACDs effectively downregulate pro-inflammatory cytokine expression while stimulating osteogenic differentiation processes in rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (rBMSCs). Mechanistic studies identified AMPK activation by ACDs as the critical pathway mediating NF-κB signal transduction inhibition, which concurrently diminishes inflammatory mediator synthesis and amplifies the bone-forming capacity of rBMSCs. ACDs administration suppressed inflammation and promoted alveolar bone regeneration. These findings present a novel strategy for developing nanodrugs with improved biocompatibility and solubility for periodontitis therapy in vivo. ( Scheme 1).

This study utilized a hydrothermal synthesis protocol employing artesunate (3 g) in combination with branched polyethyleneimine (b-PEI, 300 mg) at 200 °C for 4 h (Methods), with the resultant ACDs subsequently subjected to dialysis purification. First, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to observe the ACD nanostructure and determine the particle size distribution. TEM characterization revealed ACDs as evenly dispersed nanostructures exhibiting near-spherical morphology, with quantitative size distribution analysis confirming a mean diameter of approximately 3 nm ( Fig. 1A-B, Figure S1A). HRTEM additionally disclosed crystalline lattice structures within ACDs, displaying a well-resolved lattice distance of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1C) [27].

To investigate the chemical composition of ACDs and changes in functional groups during the carbonization process, FTIR was used to characterize ACDs and ART. FTIR analysis was subsequently performed to characterize the chemical compositions of both ACDs and ART (Fig. 1D). Spectral analysis of ACDs exhibited distinct absorption bands corresponding to O−H/N − H stretching vibrations at 3372.8 cm⁻¹ , CO groups (1697.5 cm⁻¹), conjugated CN/CC systems and N − H deformation modes (1500–1690 cm⁻¹), along with C−N/C−O functionalities (1000–1300 cm⁻¹) [28−30], confirming the presence of amine, carbonyl, and hydroxyl moieties as critical functional groups. Comparative analysis with ART’s spectral profile demonstrated the emergence of N − H vibrational modes at 3372.8 cm⁻¹ and C−N stretching vibrations within 1000–1300 cm⁻¹ , providing evidence for the integration of b-PEI-derived amine groups into ACDs through carbonization processes. Additionally, after the formation of ACDs, the ν (O−H) shifted from 3264.1 cm⁻¹ to 3372.8 cm⁻¹ , and the ν (CO) shifted from 1753.0 cm⁻¹ to 1694.5 cm⁻¹ , revealing that the carboxyl groups in ART reacted with b-PEI during the high-temperature carbonization process, leading to the chemical environment changes of the functional groups. On the basis of the above findings, we speculate that during the carbonization process, reactive functional groups such as carboxyl and amine groups from ART and b-PEI participated in the reaction, thereby promoting the formation of carbon dots.

CDs typically possess fluorescent properties, which promise the applications of CDs in bioimaging. A systematic investigation of the photophysical properties of ACDs was undertaken to evaluate their potential as biological imaging materials and to elucidate their distinctive light absorption and fluorescence characteristics (Fig. 1E, Figure S1B). Different from ART with a broad absorption band covering 200 −300 nm, ACDs exhibit an absorption peak at 208 nm, along with an absorption band in the range of 271–470 nm, which correspond to π–π* transitions and n–π*[31] transitions, respectively, in the as-prepared carbonized structures. Upon excitation at 360 nm, ACDs emit bright blue fluorescence with a peak maximum at 486 nm, whereas ART is non-emissive. The absorption and emission spectra further confirm the formation of CDs with optical properties distinct from those of ART. By laser confocal microscopy (Figure S1E), intracellular blue fluorescence was observed under the excitation of 405 nm laser, indicating successful uptake of ACDs by RAW 264.7 cells.

A comparative XPS analysis of ART and ACDs was performed to elucidate the surface elemental composition and functional group structures of ACDs. Elemental composition analysis via XPS survey spectra (Fig. 1F) revealed ACDs contain 75.67 % carbon, 7.79 % nitrogen, and 16.53 % oxygen. Notably, nitrogen incorporation was uniquely detected in ACDs, contrasting with ART’s elemental profile, which consisted exclusively of carbon (72.61 %) and oxygen (25.43 %) (Figure S1C). High-resolution spectral deconvolution identified four distinct carbon species in the C 1 s spectrum: C−C/CC (284.9 eV), C−N (285.5 eV), C−O (286.6 eV), and CO (288.3 eV) (Fig. 1G). The N 1 s spectrum resolved three nitrogen configurations: N − H (401.4 eV), graphitic nitrogen (400.3 eV), and pyrrolic nitrogen (399.1 eV) (Fig. 1H). Oxygen speciation analysis showed C−O (532.1 eV) and CO (531.0 eV) components in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1I) [32,33]. Comparative analysis with ART’s C 1 s and O 1 s spectra demonstrated reduced CO signal intensity in ACDs, suggesting these functional groups participated in the carbon dot formation process. Nitrogen doping within the graphitic framework of ACDs was confirmed through N 1 s spectral features, with surface-accessible amine functionalities detected. The results infer that the carboxyl groups in ART reacted with the amine groups in b-PEI, promoting the cross-linking of the precursors, which then further carbonized to form ACDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of ACDs for anti-inflammatory effects on LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 and promoting osteogenesis on rBMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The Gram-negative anaerobic bacterium P. gingivalis serves as a key etiological agent driving periodontitis pathogenesis [34]. This microorganism produces multiple virulence-associated molecules, such as LPS and proteolytic enzymes, which actively participate in periodontal soft tissue degradation [35]. These pathogen-derived components exhibit immunomodulatory properties, impairing host antimicrobial defenses while sustaining chronic inflammatory states [36,37]. To simulate inflammatory conditions in vitro, RAW 264.7 macrophages were stimulated with P. gingivalis-derived LPS (P.g.-LPS) in this investigation. Experimental groups comprised: untreated control (Ctrl), P.g.-LPS group (10 μg/mL), P.g.-LPS + ART (40 μg/mL), and P.g.-LPS + ACDs (40 μg/mL), with triplicate wells per condition. Given the critical role of elevated pro-inflammatory mediators IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α in periodontal disease progression and their correlation with clinical severity [38], Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) analysis demonstrated marked suppression of these cytokine transcripts following ACDs treatment ( Fig. 2A-C). Comparative assessment revealed ACDs exhibited superior anti-inflammatory efficacy compared to ART in downregulating inflammatory gene expression. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) results (Fig. 2D, S2A, S2B) were consistent with those of qRT-PCR. Previous investigations have shown the anti-inflammatory properties of ART, alleviating conditions such as colitis, sepsis, and arthritis [39]. Our results are consistent with these prior findings and demonstrate that ACDs are more effective than ART in inhibiting the expression of inflammation-associated proteins induced by LPS.

Besides anti-inflammatory effects, tissue regeneration, especially bone tissue regeneration, is also of critical importance in the treatment of periodontal diseases. Alveolar bone loss is one of the primary pathological changes in periodontitis, leading directly to tooth loosening and eventual loss. Therefore, regeneration of alveolar bone is essential to the treatment and prognosis of periodontitis [40]. Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), with their multipotent differentiation capacity and widespread tissue distribution, serve as the primary cellular source for periodontal tissue regeneration. In our study, rBMSCs were successfully isolated (Figure S3A, B) and confirmed to have multilineage differentiation capabilities.

Runt-related transcription factor 2 (RUNX2) is a pivotal transcription factor in osteogenic differentiation [41] and is the first transcription factor expressed during this process. Osterix (Sp7), a transcription factor containing a zinc-finger domain downstream of RUNX2, promotes differentiation of preosteoblasts into mature osteoblasts [42]. COL1, a major constituent of the bone organic matrix, serves as a marker of early-stage osteogenic differentiation [43]. Concurrently, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) catalyzes pyrophosphate hydrolysis, providing phosphate for hydroxyapatite formation and serving as a functional indicator of osteogenic potential [44]. The molecular markers RUNX2, Sp7, COL1, and ALP demonstrate elevated expression during the initial stages of osteoblast differentiation, making them suitable indicators for evaluating early osteogenic differentiation in rBMSCs. Six experimental groups were established: untreated control (Ctrl), LPS, LPS + ACDs, LPS + ART, ACDs-only, and ART-only. qRT-PCR analysis (Fig. 2E-H) revealed that after 72-hour treatment, the LPS + ACDs group displayed substantially increased expression of osteogenesis-related genes compared to both LPS-treated and LPS+ART groups. Under physiological conditions, gene expression levels in ACDs-treated cells exceeded those observed in either the Ctrl or ART groups. These data collectively demonstrate the superior capacity of ACDs to upregulate osteogenic gene expression in rBMSCs across both inflammatory and non-inflammatory environments compared with ART. Subsequent ALP staining evaluation of osteogenic differentiation revealed enhanced staining intensity and increased ALP-positive cell counts in the LPS + ACDs group versus the LPS group at days 7 and 14 (Fig. 2I-K), indicating amplified ALP activity under inflammatory stimulation. Similarly, under normal culture conditions, ACDs-treated rBMSCs exhibited significantly greater ALP enzymatic activity than untreated controls, confirming ACDs’ dual functionality in promoting osteogenic differentiation under varying physiological states.

Calcium deposition is a key marker of osteoblast maturation. Alizarin red staining binds to calcium deposits in the extracellular matrix and produces a red color; therefore, it was selected to evaluate the late-stage osteogenic differentiation ability of rBMSCs. Following a 21-day osteogenic induction period, quantitative analysis of Alizarin red-stained cultures (Fig. 2L, M) demonstrated increased mineralized nodule formation in the LPS + ACDs group relative to LPS-treated counterparts. Under non-inflammatory conditions, ACDs-exposed rBMSCs displayed significantly greater calcified nodule deposition compared to untreated controls. These collective observations confirm ACDs capacity to enhance terminal osteogenic differentiation processes in rBMSCs across varying physiological environments.

Collectively, these findings demonstrate that ACDs significantly enhance osteogenic differentiation capacity in rBMSCs across both inflammatory and physiological microenvironments, demonstrating superior efficacy compared to ART in promoting bone formation processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ACDs ameliorated lipopolysaccharide LPS-induced inflammation and significantly facilitated bone regeneration via AMPK activation
ce:section.ce:para: Functioning as a heterotrimeric serine/threonine kinase, AMPK regulates cellular energy balance by suppressing ATP-depleting pathways and stimulating energy-producing metabolic processes [45]. This kinase also modulates critical biological responses, including inflammatory signaling, oxidative stress regulation, and neural degeneration under metabolic challenges [46]. To investigate the AMPK-mediated anti-inflammatory mechanism of ACDs in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages, qRT-PCR and Western blot analyses were conducted to evaluate cytokine expression changes following AMPK-targeting siRNA transfection. The fluorescence detection serves to verify whether siRNA has been successfully transfected into the cells, and the fluorescent signals exhibited in Figure S4 provide clear evidence of the successful transfection. To initially investigate the effects of AMPK siRNA, we conducted experiments divided into four groups: control (Ctrl), LPS, LPS + AMPK siRNA, and AMPK siRNA. As shown in Figure S5, the expression levels of IL-6 and TNF-α were significantly elevated in the LPS, LPS + AMPK siRNA, and AMPK siRNA groups compared to the control group. Notably, the LPS + AMPK siRNA group exhibited higher levels of IL-6 and TNF-α than the LPS group. These findings indicate that AMPK siRNA successfully blocked the AMPK signaling pathway, regardless of the inflammatory state, and promoted the inflammatory response. The experimental groups included: untreated control (Ctrl), LPS, LPS + ART, LPS + ACDs, and LPS + ACDs + AMPK siRNA. Both qRT-PCR (Figures S6A-B) and immunoblotting ( Fig. 3A) demonstrated upregulated TNF-α (Fig. 3B) and IL-6 (Fig. 3C) expression in AMPK-silenced cells compared to the LPS + ACDs group, confirming AMPK pathway dependency for ACDs anti-inflammatory efficacy. Comparative phosphoprotein analysis (Fig. 3D) revealed diminished AMPK phosphorylation (p-AMPK) levels in LPS-treated cells versus controls (Fig. 3E). ACDs treatment markedly enhanced p-AMPK levels, while AMPK siRNA transfection reversed this effect, confirming ACDs-mediated AMPK activation under inflammatory conditions. These findings align with established literature demonstrating AMPK-mediated downregulation of inflammatory cytokines [47,48].

The NF-κB signaling cascade represents a canonical inflammation-regulating pathway, with AMPK-mediated phosphorylation demonstrated to suppress NF-κB activation [49]. Prior studies have established ART’s capacity to inhibit NF-κB transcriptional activity, thereby curbing the production and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines in monocytic and macrophage lineages [50]. Core regulatory elements of this pathway include P65 and IκBα, whose phosphorylation status serves as biochemical markers of NF-κB pathway activation [51,52]. Under physiological conditions, NF-κB/P65 is sequestered in the cytoplasm through complex formation with IκBα, maintaining pathway quiescence. Inflammatory triggers activate the IκB kinase (IKK) complex, which orchestrates residue-specific phosphorylation of IκBα at Ser³ ²/³ ⁶ residues. This post-translational modification induces P65 dissociation and subsequent nuclear migration to mediate transcriptional regulation [53]. Scientific evidence confirms the pivotal role of cytoplasmic-to-nuclear translocation of NF-κB/P65 in pathway activation, highlighting the therapeutic potential of pharmacological targeting of this nucleocytoplasmic transport [54]. To elucidate AMPK’s regulatory influence on NF-κB dynamics, the phosphorylation status of both P65 and IκBα was systematically analyzed. Western blot analysis (Fig. 3D) showed that levels of phosphorylated IκBα (p-IκBα) (Fig. 3F) and phosphorylated P65 (p-P65) (Fig. 3G) were significantly reduced in the LPS + ACDs group, indicating that ACDs inhibited LPS-induced NF-κB activation. Moreover, in the LPS + ACDs + AMPK siRNA group, levels of p-IκBα and p-P65 were elevated again, suggesting that LPS-induced NF-κB activation occurs via the AMPK pathway. Collectively, these results indicate that the anti-inflammatory efficacy of ACDs in periodontitis therapy is mediated through the enhancement of AMPK phosphorylation within RAW 264.7 macrophages. This molecular mechanism facilitates downregulation of the NF-κB cascade, culminating in attenuated phosphorylation events of both P65 and IκBα, ultimately suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine production.

Multiple regulatory molecules have been shown to modulate periodontal inflammatory responses and bone metabolism through AMPK-dependent mechanisms [55,56]. Existing research has revealed that creatine enhances osteogenic differentiation capacity in dental pulp stem cells through activation of the AMPK-ULK1-autophagy regulatory axis [57]. Furthermore, exosomes derived from human deciduous tooth stem cells (SHED-exosomes) potentially stimulate angiogenic and osteogenic processes via AMPK-mediated signaling, thereby supporting periodontal bone reconstruction [58]. In this investigation, AMPK phosphorylation status was assessed through Western blot analysis (Fig. 3H). Quantitative data (Fig. 3I) demonstrated elevated p-AMPK levels in both LPS + ACDs-treated cells relative to LPS counterparts, and in ACDs-treated cells compared to untreated controls, confirming AMPK phosphorylation enhancement by ACDs under both inflammatory and physiological conditions. To study the relationship between AMPK and osteogenesis, cells were divided into four experimental groups: LPS + ACDs, LPS + ACDs + AMPK inhibitor (Compound C), ACDs, and ACDs + AMPK inhibitor (Compound C). Immunoblotting data (Fig. 3J–N) revealed a marked attenuation in protein abundance of osteogenesis-associated markers mediated by ACDs in rBMSCs following pharmacological inhibition of the AMPK cascade, with comparable suppression observed under both inflammatory and physiological culture conditions.

Collectively, these experimental data substantiate that ACDs enhance the osteogenic differentiation capacity of rBMSCs across both inflammatory and physiological microenvironments through AMPK pathway activation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ACDs reduce tissue damage in a rat model of ligation-induced periodontitis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the therapeutic potential of ACDs in combating inflammation and enhancing bone formation, a rodent periodontitis model was created through ligature placement around the maxillary first molar with subsequent local administration [59]. After 14 days of treatment, experimental animals underwent humane sacrifice for specimen collection. Three-dimensional reconstructed images visually demonstrated the extent of alveolar bone resorption ( Fig. 4A). Morphometric evaluation of periodontal tissue degradation was performed through vertical measurements between the cementoenamel junction (CEJ) and alveolar bone crest (ABC) [60]. Morphometric analysis (Fig. 4B) indicated reduced CEJ-ABC dimensions in ACDs-treated samples compared to ligation-induced controls. Three-dimensional micro-CT parameters (bone volume fraction (BV/TV), trabecular separation (Tb. Sp), and trabecular thickness (Tb. Th) were quantitatively assessed using CTan software. Comparative analysis of these volumetric parameters (Fig. 4C-E) revealed enhanced osteogenic potential of ACDs relative to ART intervention.

To validate these observations, the anti-inflammatory efficacy of ACDs was investigated in rat maxillary molar interdental periodontal tissues through histological approaches. Histopathological evaluation was conducted through hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and Masson trichrome staining methodologies. In the control group, alveolar bone morphology remained intact, with uniform mineralization, continuous trabeculae, and no evident resorption at the alveolar crest. In contrast, the periodontitis group exhibited a marked decrease in alveolar bone height, accompanied by abundant inflammatory cells (Fig. 4F, S7; black arrow) within the periodontal tissue between the distal middle root of the first molar and the proximal middle root of the second molar. These observations correspond to the clinical manifestations of periodontitis. Therapeutic administration of ACDs significantly attenuated these pathological alterations relative to periodontitis controls with a marked reduction in inflammatory cells (Fig. 4F-G). Fig. 4H, I showed that, in the Perio group, the blue staining area of collagen fibers was reduced, indicating that the local inflammatory response disrupted collagen fibers; in contrast, more blue areas were observed in the ACDs group. These findings collectively demonstrate that ACDs reduce collagen destruction, promote alveolar bone regeneration, and accelerate recovery from periodontitis.

To explore the clinical applicability of experimental observations, the therapeutic effects of ACDs on pro-inflammatory cytokine generation and osteogenic marker expression were systematically evaluated. Quantification of inflammatory mediators was performed using IHC techniques in vivo. During periodontitis progression, microbial pathogens in dental plaque biofilms initiate the secretion of inflammatory mediators, including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α, within periodontal tissues, which subsequently trigger progressive tissue breakdown [61]. Quantification of inflammatory activity involved calculating the positively stained area percentages for TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β in IHC specimens. Analytical data ( Fig. 5A–D) demonstrated significantly lower immunoreactivity for TNF-α (Fig. 5B), IL-6 (Fig. 5C), and IL-1β (Fig. 5D) in ACDs-treated specimens relative to both ligation-induced and ART-treated groups, indicating superior anti-inflammatory efficacy of ACDs in periodontal contexts. IHC staining of osteogenic-related factors (Fig. 5A) showed that, compared with the ligation and ART groups, the areas positive for RUNX2 (Fig. 5E) and OPN (Fig. 5F) in periodontal tissues from the ACDs group were significantly increased. Taken together, these findings indicate that ACDs effectively reduce periodontal inflammation and promote osteogenesis.

ART exhibits poor water solubility, which restricts its clinical application. Conversion of ART into carbon dots markedly enhances solubility and thus improves drug bioavailability. Beyond augmenting ART efficacy, carbon dots function as a delivery platform for targeted and controlled release, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcome. Their intrinsic optical properties further enable imaging-guided intervention. In vivo studies confirm that ACDs exert significant effects in animal models. Based on the advantages of ACDs, we believe that they also hold potential for application in humans. However, a comprehensive evaluation of human safety, encompassing long-term toxicity and potential immunogenicity, remains necessary. Future work should include expanded preclinical assessments and clinical trials to determine ACDs pharmacokinetics, safety profile, and sustained efficacy in humans. Integration with emerging modalities such as gene therapy and tissue engineering may yield combination strategies using ACDs as carriers, offering a novel avenue for personalized periodontitis treatment.

Cell biocompatibility was evaluated by measuring cell viability and cell number via the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Experimental data demonstrated ACDs at concentrations ranging from 0 to 60 μg/mL exhibited sustained cellular viability (>90 %) in RAW 264.7 macrophages following a 24-hour incubation period (Figure S8). In contrast, when ART concentration reached 50–60 μg/mL, cell viability decreased by approximately 20 % after 24 h, suggesting that ACDs possess better biocompatibility than ART. Live/dead staining, which distinguishes viable cells from nonviable ones through specific staining protocols, was utilized to assess the overall cell viability. Live/dead cell staining (Figure S9) showed no significant increase in red fluorescence of dead cells within the 0–60 μg/mL range, and cells adhered well with no notable changes in morphology or behavior. Cell cycle and apoptosis assays (Figures S10A–B) revealed that ACDs (0–60 μg/mL) had little impact on the cell cycle or apoptosis of RAW 264.7 cells after 24 h, compared with the blank control group. Histopathological evaluation of heart, liver, spleen, and kidney tissues from in vivo experiments showed normal architecture and cellular morphology in both ACDs and ART groups, comparable to controls (Figure S11). Body weight was measured every two days, and the results (Figure S12) showed minimal differences among the groups. In conclusion, ACDs display low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.

In this study, we synthesized ACDs via a hydrothermal method. The ACDs exhibited both anti-inflammatory properties and osteogenic potential. Compared with ART, ACDs demonstrated lower toxicity and superior water solubility, resulting in enhanced biocompatibility and broader applicability. Using a RAW 264.7 inflammation model, we found that ACDs more effectively suppressed inflammatory factor expression under both inflammatory and normal conditions than ART. In an osteogenesis model with rBMSCs, ACDs promoted calcium nodule formation and enhanced expression of osteogenic genes and proteins, whereas ART lacked osteogenic properties. Mechanistic studies elucidated that ACDs selectively modulated AMPK activation while suppressing the NF-κB cascade, consequently attenuating inflammatory mediator production and enhancing osteoblastic differentiation in rBMSCs. Evaluations using a murine periodontitis model confirmed the capacity of ACDs to mitigate periodontal inflammation while facilitating alveolar bone neoformation in vivo. Collectively, these findings establish the ACDs developed in this investigation as promising candidates for periodontitis therapeutics. Future studies will investigate ACDs catabolism in animal models to enhance the reliability of this research. Given ACDs capacity to promote osteogenesis under non-inflammatory conditions, treatment of non-inflammatory bone diseases represents a prospective research direction.

Di Li: Methodology, Investigation. Rui Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Shuo Yu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology. Jinzhong Liu: Conceptualization. Xiaolin Ma: Software. Ziqing Wei: Resources. Huiling Ye: Visualization. Liangheng Zou: Investigation. Dandan Liu: Conceptualization. Shanlin Qu: Writing – review & editing. Zhihui Liu: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that the research was conducted in the absence of any commercial or financial relationships that could be construed as a potential conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2025.114873.
